original_abstract: Abstract Spatial transcriptomics technologies generate gene expression profiles with spatial context, requiring spatially informed analysis tools for three key tasks, spatial clustering, multisample integration, and cell-type deconvolution. We present GraphST, a graph self-supervised contrastive learning method that fully exploits spatial transcriptomics data to outperform existing methods. It combines graph neural networks with self-supervised contrastive learning to learn informative and discriminative spot representations by minimizing the embedding distance between spatially adjacent spots and vice versa. We demonstrated GraphST on multiple tissue types and technology platforms. GraphST achieved 10% higher clustering accuracy and better delineated fine-grained tissue structures in brain and embryo tissues. GraphST is also the only method that can jointly analyze multiple tissue slices in vertical or horizontal integration while correcting batch effects. Lastly, GraphST demonstrated superior cell-type deconvolution to capture spatial niches like lymph node germinal centers and exhausted tumor infiltrating T cells in breast tumor tissue.
humanized_abstract: Summary Spatial transcriptomics is an emerging generation of genomics methods that generate gene expression profiles with spatial information. Analyzing the resulting spatial data effectively for three fundamental analyses — spatial clustering, multisample integration, and cell-type deconvolution — therefore demands new approaches that utilize the unique spatial information present in the data. We present a new method named GraphST that uses graph self-supervised contrastive learning to utilize the spatial information to superior results for all the three tasks. Our method, GraphST, utilizes the graph neural networks combined with the concept of self-supervised contrastive learning to learn the representations of individual gene expression values in each transcriptomic spot, such that the representations of the neighboring spots are as close as possible and vice versa, yielding informative and discriminative representations for the analysis of spatial transcriptomics data. We demonstrate the versatility and strong performance of GraphST on diverse tissue types and spatial transcriptomics data from different technology platforms. In comprehensive experiments with various public datasets, GraphST achieves 10% higher clustering accuracy, reveals fine grain information of tissue structures, such as the detailed patterns of the brain and embryo, achieves the first joint vertical or horizontal analysis of multislice tissue samples while removing batch effects, and recovers spatial organization of diverse cell-types, including accurately modeling the spatial germinal centers in lymph nodes and exhausted T cells in tumor-infiltrating the breast tumors.
